title: create pod on the fly
body:
when a user specifies --pod to podman create|run, we should create that pod
automatically.  the port bindings from the container are then inherited by
the infra container.  this signicantly improves the workflow of running
containers inside pods with podman.  the user is still encouraged to use
podman pod create to have more granular control of the pod create options.

Signed-off-by: baude <bbaude@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

I am not a huge fan of side effects like this.  The biggest problem I have with them, is it allows typos to succeed and the user has no idea what went wrong.
Why not just inform the user to add the port if it does not exist.

pod foobar does not exist.  Create the foobar pod by executing `podman pod create foobar`.

because that isnt the end of the story.  the user then has to go extract the port bindings from the container image and add them to the pod creation.  if you are worried about typos, consider the opportunity to mess that up (or the user forgets to add ports, or doesnt know we cannot add ports to a pod afterwards).  then the user has to delete the pod (and potentially container) and start over.

Sadly, I guess I got to agree with you. But we have that type of issue all over the place. 
Of course if the container fails to be created, it could cause issues, since now the user need to clean up the pod.

But I will go along with it in this case.  

Should we fail if the user creates a container with a pod that exists and the user specified exposed ports, and tell them that will not work.  Or will it work in libpod but not in Kubernetes?

I really don't like this. What happens if I typo "pod1" as "pod2" when trying to add a container to a pod, and end up making an entirely new pod (and being really confused as to why my new container can't talk to anything else in the pod)?

If we are going to do this - we really ought to have a special sigil of some sort for creating and not joining pods. --pod=new:<name> or something.

And we need to refactor the pod create code to only happen in one place, like @rhatdan said

Yes, I kind of like --pod=new, and then the pod would be named the same as the container.

podman create --pod=new --name walsh ...

You end up with a container and a pod named walsh.

This then requires that the user must pass a container name or we have to fail them because we dont have a auto-generated container name until the container is created; and once the container is created, it cannot be added to the pod.  i also dont love when we use the command input to be meaningful like netmode is ... meaning what the user puts in could be a name or some other type but if this is a strong requirement, i'll play along.
@baude Eh, I'd be fine with them passing in the name too... Maybe --pod=new:pod_name or similar?
I would go allong with --pod new:name
:umbrella: The latest upstream changes (presumably #1896) made this pull request unmergeable. Please resolve the merge conflicts.
